caseStudy.overview: ProMat 2025 was a major industry trade show in Chicago and a significant moment for Agility Robotics — the debut of the beta version of Digit's 4th generation. As the company's sole graphic designer, I owned the visual design across every touchpoint at the booth: large-format wall graphics, digital LED panel content, printed collateral, and team apparel.
caseStudy.team: Graphic Designer (me), Director of Design, CMO, Event Coordinator, Booth Manufacturer
objectives: Debut the beta version of Digit Gen 4 in a compelling, credible way
Drive potential customer interest and engagement on the show floor
Reinforce Agility's position as the leader in commercial humanoid robotics
Communicate that Digit is real, working technology — not vaporware
caseStudy.challenge: This was my first time designing for print at this scale. Every asset — wall graphics, LED panels, handouts, apparel — had to work cohesively across very different formats and contexts, all while being delivered weeks before the show to allow for production. Booth placement on the edge of the show floor also meant some walls may get less visibility than expected, which I only fully appreciated in hindsight.
caseStudy.approach: I started by reviewing Agility's previous two ProMat booths, neither of which I had designed. My direction ended up being substantially different from both. I prioritized the robot above everything else — the visual language had to lead with Digit, not with words or abstract brand elements. I worked directly with the booth manufacturer's in-house designer, who provided dimension templates for each wall panel. For apparel, I collaborated with our event coordinator to design something people would actually want to wear: subtle branding with technical robot artwork rather than heavy logo treatment.

userExperience.description: Trade show attendees are overwhelmed with competing stimuli. The design challenge was to cut through the noise at a distance, then reward closer engagement. I also had to consider the live demo context — robots would be actively working in the booth, so the interior walls needed to stay visually quiet to keep focus on Digit in motion.
insights: Large-scale robot renders are more eye-catching from a distance than text or abstract graphics
Keeping interior walls plain ensures the robots remain the focal point during live demos
Apparel that people genuinely want to wear extends brand presence beyond the booth floor

caseStudy.solution: The final booth design centered on large-scale, high-resolution renders of Digit on the perimeter walls — bold enough to draw attention from across the show floor. Digital LED panels featured motion assets. Printed handouts included a spec sheet and a personality-driven 'resume' for Digit. Team apparel featured a minimal Agility logo alongside detailed technical artwork of the robot, making it something crew members still wear around the office after the show.

results: High foot traffic to the booth despite edge-of-floor placement
Renders printed at large scale with accurate color reproduction and sufficient resolution
Booth design viewed as comparable in quality and polish to surrounding booths
Team apparel received strong positive feedback — crew necks are still worn in the office
The show itself was a success in debuting Digit Gen 4 to potential customers and the industry
